course_info.title: Health Information Systems
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Map the full landscape of clinical and administrative information systems in a health system
Understand the integration architecture connecting these systems
Evaluate vendor strategies and market dynamics
readings: Health Information Systems: Managing Clinical Care Information Ch. 1-2
KLAS Research: Healthcare IT Market Overview 2024

lecture_notes.introduction: A modern health system may operate hundreds of information systems. Understanding this landscape and how systems interrelate is essential for planning, integration, and optimization.
key_concepts: Core clinical systems: EHR, CPOE, eMAR, clinical documentation
Ancillary systems: LIS, RIS, PACS, pharmacy, blood bank
Administrative systems: ADT, scheduling, revenue cycle, supply chain
Integration platforms: interface engines, ESBs, FHIR servers
Best-of-breed vs. single-vendor strategy tradeoffs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Interactive architecture diagram of a 500-bed hospital showing all major systems and their integration points

review.value: Best-of-breed selects the top system per domain but requires complex integration; single vendor (e.g., Epic) simplifies integration but may sacrifice specialty functionality
Admission-Discharge-Transfer system managing patient movements - the authoritative source for patient location and census

class_schedule.topic: System Development Life Cycle in Healthcare
learning_objectives: Apply SDLC phases to healthcare IT projects
Compare waterfall, agile, and hybrid methodologies in health IT
Understand regulatory constraints on health IT development
readings: ONC Health IT Playbook: System Selection and Implementation

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare IT projects must balance speed with safety. The SDLC provides structure for managing complex system implementations where patient safety is at stake.
key_concepts: SDLC phases: planning, analysis, design, implementation, maintenance
Waterfall: appropriate for regulatory-driven projects with fixed requirements
Agile: iterative development for evolving clinical needs
Requirements gathering in healthcare: clinical workflow analysis, stakeholder interviews
Validation and verification for patient safety-critical systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study of an agile EHR module development project, walking through sprint planning, user stories, and clinical validation

review.value: Iterative approach delivering working software in short sprints, requiring clinical stakeholder involvement in every sprint review
Systematic testing of health IT systems by clinical end users to verify they support safe and effective care workflows

learning_objectives: Explain how integration engines route and transform healthcare messages
Describe HL7 v2 message structure and common message types
Configure basic message routing rules in an integration engine

lecture_notes.introduction: Integration engines are the nervous system of healthcare IT. They route millions of messages daily between systems that would otherwise be unable to communicate.
key_concepts: Integration engine architecture: channels, filters, transformers, destinations
HL7 v2 message structure: MSH, PID, PV1, OBR, OBX segments
Common message types: ADT (A01, A08, A03), ORM, ORU, SIU
Message routing: content-based routing, filtering, transformation
Error handling: dead letter queues, retry logic, alerting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configure a channel in Mirth Connect to receive an ADT A01 message, transform patient demographics, and route to a downstream system
setup_requirements: Mirth Connect or similar integration engine installed
Sample HL7 v2 messages for testing

review.value: Admit/Visit Notification message sent when a patient is admitted, containing demographics, visit info, and insurance data
Middleware that receives, routes, transforms, and delivers messages between healthcare systems using configurable channels

learning_objectives: Design RESTful integrations using FHIR resources
Implement SMART on FHIR application launch and authorization
Compare FHIR integration patterns with traditional HL7 v2 messaging

lecture_notes.introduction: FHIR is transforming healthcare integration from batch messaging to real-time, API-driven data exchange. Understanding FHIR implementation is a critical skill.
key_concepts: FHIR resource model: Patient, Encounter, Condition, Observation, MedicationRequest
RESTful operations: read, search, create, update on FHIR resources
SMART on FHIR: OAuth2 authorization for clinical apps within the EHR
FHIR Subscriptions for event-driven integration
US Core Implementation Guide: required profiles and must-support elements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a simple SMART on FHIR app that launches from an EHR context, retrieves patient data, and displays medication history

review.value: Standards-based framework for building apps that launch from within EHRs using OAuth2 for authorization and FHIR for data access
FHIR implementation guide defining minimum required data elements for US healthcare interoperability, mandated by ONC

class_schedule.topic: Ancillary System Integration: Laboratory and Imaging
learning_objectives: Describe LIS and RIS/PACS integration with the EHR
Understand DICOM standards for medical imaging
Evaluate interoperability challenges in diagnostic workflows

lecture_notes.introduction: Laboratory and imaging systems are among the most critical ancillary systems in healthcare. Their integration with the EHR directly impacts diagnostic accuracy and turnaround time.
key_concepts: LIS integration: order transmission (ORM), result delivery (ORU), LOINC coding
RIS/PACS integration: DICOM standard for image management and communication
Radiology workflow: order, schedule, acquire, read, report
Results delivery: critical value alerting and provider notification
Vendor neutral archive (VNA) and enterprise imaging strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace a lab order from CPOE through interface engine to LIS, result processing, and return to EHR results review
setup_requirements: Integration engine with sample lab interfaces

review.value: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine - standard for transmitting, storing, and sharing medical images across systems
Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes - universal standard for identifying lab tests and clinical observations

class_schedule.topic: System Monitoring, Maintenance, and Disaster Recovery
learning_objectives: Design monitoring strategies for healthcare IT systems
Develop disaster recovery and business continuity plans
Understand downtime procedures and their clinical impact
readings: NIST Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare systems must operate 24/7. When they fail, patient safety is at risk. Monitoring, maintenance, and disaster recovery planning are not optional.
key_concepts: System monitoring: uptime, response time, error rates, queue depths
Alerting strategies: paging, escalation, automated remediation
Disaster recovery: RPO (recovery point objective), RTO (recovery time objective)
Business continuity: downtime procedures, paper-based workflows, communication plans
Lessons from major health IT outages and their patient safety impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a monitoring dashboard for a hospital integration engine showing key metrics and alert thresholds

review.value: RPO defines maximum acceptable data loss (time); RTO defines maximum acceptable system downtime - both must be defined for every critical system
Pre-planned paper-based or alternative workflows activated when critical systems are unavailable, requiring regular testing and staff training

questions.question: A hospital is choosing between a best-of-breed IT strategy (selecting the top system per domain) and a single-vendor strategy (e.g., Epic for all modules). Analyze the tradeoffs from an integration, cost, and clinical functionality perspective.
questions.answer: Best-of-breed advantages: specialized functionality optimized for each domain (e.g., best LIS for labs, best PACS for radiology), ability to replace individual components. Disadvantages: requires building and maintaining many interfaces, higher integration costs, potential for data silos, multiple vendor relationships. Single-vendor advantages: native integration between modules, single data model, unified user experience, lower integration costs, one vendor to manage. Disadvantages: may sacrifice domain-specific depth, vendor lock-in, negotiating leverage decreases, system-wide outages affect everything. The trend is toward single-vendor with selective best-of-breed for specialized areas.

rubric.key: Cost and clinical functionality tradeoffs addressed

questions.question: Explain the structure of an HL7 v2 ADT A01 message. Identify at least 4 segments and describe what data each contains.
questions.answer: An HL7 v2 ADT A01 (Admit/Visit Notification) message contains: (1) MSH - Message Header: sending/receiving application, message type, timestamp, encoding. (2) PID - Patient Identification: MRN, name, DOB, gender, address, SSN, race, language. (3) PV1 - Patient Visit: patient class (inpatient), assigned location (unit/bed), attending physician, admission type, admit date. (4) NK1 - Next of Kin: emergency contact name, relationship, phone. (5) IN1 - Insurance: plan ID, group number, subscriber information. (6) GT1 - Guarantor: financially responsible party information.

questions.question: Describe the SMART on FHIR app launch sequence. What role does OAuth2 play, and why is this approach preferred over direct database access for third-party clinical applications?
questions.answer: SMART on FHIR launch sequence: (1) User launches app from EHR context, (2) EHR redirects to app's launch URL with context parameters, (3) App requests authorization from EHR's OAuth2 server, (4) User/admin approves scopes (patient/*.read, etc.), (5) App receives access token, (6) App uses token to call FHIR APIs for patient data. OAuth2 provides delegated authorization with scoped access. This is preferred over direct database access because: it enforces the principle of least privilege, provides audit trails, allows patients and admins to control what data is shared, uses standard protocols reducing custom integration, and prevents direct database connections that bypass EHR security controls.

rubric.key: Valid reasons for preferring this approach

questions.question: Trace a laboratory order from physician entry to result display, identifying every system and interface involved.
questions.answer: Flow: (1) Physician enters lab order in EHR (CPOE), (2) EHR generates HL7 ORM message, (3) Integration engine receives and routes ORM to LIS, (4) LIS processes order, generates specimen labels with barcodes, (5) Phlebotomist collects specimen, scans barcode linking specimen to order, (6) Lab analyzer processes specimen and sends results to LIS, (7) Lab technologist reviews and verifies results in LIS, (8) LIS generates HL7 ORU message with LOINC-coded results, (9) Integration engine routes ORU back to EHR, (10) EHR displays results in patient chart, (11) If critical value, automated alert notifies ordering provider.

rubric.key: Complete workflow from order to result
All systems correctly identified (EHR, integration engine, LIS)
HL7 message types and standards referenced

major_assignments.title: Health Information Systems Integration Project
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a health information systems integration solution for a multi-facility health system, including architecture design, interface specifications, FHIR API implementation, and testing plan.
objectives: Design an enterprise integration architecture for healthcare
Implement HL7 v2 and FHIR-based interfaces
Document the solution for technical and clinical stakeholders
deliverables: Interface specification documents for 3 key interfaces
Working FHIR API queries demonstrating data retrieval
Integration test plan with test cases

criteria: Working HL7 v2 or FHIR interfaces
Working interface implementations with standards compliance

major_assignments.title: System Monitoring and Disaster Recovery Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive monitoring strategy and disaster recovery plan for a hospital's critical information systems, including monitoring dashboards, alerting rules, recovery procedures, and downtime workflows.
objectives: Design system monitoring for healthcare IT infrastructure
Define RPO and RTO for each critical system
Create downtime procedures for clinical operations
deliverables: Downtime procedure quick-reference cards for clinical staff
Tabletop exercise scenario and facilitator guide

criteria: Appropriate KPIs selected for each system
RPO/RTO defined for all critical systems
Recovery procedures are detailed and actionable
Clear and concise for use under stress
Comprehensive monitoring strategy with measurable KPIs
Detailed and actionable disaster recovery plan

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of systems knowledge, integration concepts, and standards

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on labs with integration engines, FHIR APIs, and monitoring tools

major_assignments.description: Integration project and disaster recovery plan

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of systems landscape and integration standards

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including FHIR, ancillary systems, and operations

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. Lecture attendance expected. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Lab deliverables due within one week of lab session. No extensions without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in teams of 2. Major assignments are individual unless designated as group projects.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All integration code and documentation must be original work. Open-source tools may be used with proper attribution.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with integration engine (Mirth Connect), FHIR client tool, HTTP client (Postman), and architecture diagramming software.
